uavs.name: Predator / Gray Eagle

variants.description: ACTD air vehicle, Rotax 912 UL engine
Block 1 air vehicle, Rotax 914 UL, de-icing

uavs.description: The Predator was the U.S. Air Force's first operational tactical multimission UAV. GA-ASI was awarded an ACTD contract by DARPA in January 1994 for the Tier II MAE (Medium Altitude Endurance) UAV requirement. Based on the earlier Gnat-750, it first flew in July 1994 and was first used operationally in July 1995 in Albania. It was extensively used in Afghanistan and Iraq. The final USAF Predator was delivered in 2011 (250+ aircraft), and the last MQ-1s were retired in March 2018. The MQ-1C Gray Eagle is the U.S. Army's long-endurance armed variant, with automatic take-off and landing (ATLS). By 2019, the Army had procured 204 MQ-1Cs. The GE 25M variant adds a new radar, enhanced ECM resilience, and dual-rail missile launchers for up to 8 Hellfires.
uavs.notes: The Mojave / Gray Eagle STOL variant (turboprop, 7 hardpoints) was unveiled in 2021 for austere-field/amphibious operations but has not received firm U.S. military orders.

uavs.name: Pioneer
uavs.manufacturer: IAI / Pioneer UAV Inc. (IAI + AAI joint venture)

RQ-2A.propulsion: Sachs & Fichtel SF2-350 piston, 19.4 kW (26 hp); RQ-2C: UEL AR-741 rotary, 28.3 kW (38 hp)

variants.description: Improved with UCARS and MIAG, ordered 1997; 33 RQ-2As upgraded
Planned upgrade with AR-741 engine and IR laser designator (few, if any, converted)
Flycatcher — target drone conversion of surplus airframes

uavs.description: The Pioneer was the first tactical battlefield UAV in service with the U.S. armed forces. IAI began development in 1984; the Pioneer won a U.S. Navy fly-off in January 1986. It saw major combat in Operation Desert Storm (1991) — during one famous incident, Iraqi soldiers surrendered to a Pioneer drone. The Navy deployed Pioneers from Iowa-class battleships and Austin-class amphibious ships. Retired in 2007, replaced by the RQ-8 Fire Scout (delayed). Surviving aircraft were converted to QRQ-2B Flycatcher target drones.
uavs.notes: The Pioneer significantly contributed to growing military recognition of UAVs as combat assets in the 1990s.

uavs.name: DarkStar
uavs.description: DarkStar was a stealthy, highly autonomous HALE UAV developed under DARPA's Tier III Minus ACTD program to operate in contested airspace. It crashed on its second flight in April 1996 and was redesigned. Performance was consistently disappointing and costs overran. The program was cancelled in January 1999 before entering operational service.
uavs.notes: Designed to be fully autonomous and able to penetrate heavily defended airspace with its flying-wing low-observable design.

uavs.name: Global Hawk / Triton
uavs.status: Active (phasing out by 2027 USAF) / Active (USN MQ-4C)

variants.description: 5 x Block 20 converted for BACN (Battlefield Airborne Communications Node); retired 2021
Phoenix — NATO Alliance Ground Surveillance, Block 40 based; IOC Feb 2021
Euro Hawk — German Bundeswehr (SIGINT, EADS payload); cancelled after 1 aircraft
Triton — U.S. Navy BAMS maritime surveillance; IOC Sept 2023
Triton for German Bundeswehr PEGASUS program — cancelled 2020

uavs.description: Global Hawk is the USAF's first operational HAE UAV. DARPA issued the Tier II+ requirement in 1994; Teledyne Ryan won the ACTD contract in March 1995. First flew February 1998. It can survey 100,000 km² per day and fly intercontinental distances. Used operationally over Afghanistan, Iraq, North Korea. ~45 RQ-4A/B built; USAF plans retirement by 2027. MQ-4C Triton achieved IOC with USN in September 2023; 27 planned.
uavs.notes: The RQ-4B Block 40 features the AN/ZPY-2 MP-RTIP AESA radar for wide-area ground moving target surveillance. The USAF retirement plan is interpreted as evidence that classified successors like the alleged 'RQ-180' are already operational.

uavs.name: Hunter
uavs.description: A joint U.S.-Israeli UAV serving the U.S. Army. Originally designated BQM-155, it was re-designated RQ-5A under the new Q-for-UAV system. The MQ-5B variant was armed with the Viper Strike precision-guided munition. The Hunter was replaced by the MQ-1C Gray Eagle.
uavs.notes: Was also developed as the Hunter II (enlarged derivative) as a competitor to the MQ-1C ERMP program, which it lost to GA-ASI's Sky Warrior.

uavs.name: Outrider

variants.description: Only variant; cancelled before full production

uavs.description: The Outrider was developed as a tactical TUAV for the U.S. Army and Navy. After persistent performance deficiencies and cost overruns in 1997/98, the Army conducted a competitive fly-off between the Outrider and Shadow 200. The Shadow 200 won, and the RQ-6 program was cancelled in 1999. The Navy also withdrew from the program, instead choosing the RQ-8 Fire Scout VTUAV.
uavs.notes: Cancellation triggered the selection of the RQ-7 Shadow 200 as the primary U.S. Army TUAV.

uavs.name: Shadow 200

variants.description: Block 1 — initial production, IAI Tamam POP EO/IR payload
Block 2 — larger wings, 7h endurance, upgraded avionics, IMU, TCDL
Further improved endurance and payload options

uavs.description: The Shadow 200 short-range TUAV was developed by AAI in the 1990s. It won the 1999 U.S. Army TUAV competition over the RQ-6 Outrider, receiving its first LRIP contract. The system consists of 4 UAVs + 2 GCSs with both LOS and non-LOS datalinks. Approved for full-rate production in October 2002. A system consists of four air vehicles. Extensively used in Iraq and Afghanistan for reconnaissance, surveillance, target acquisition, and BDA.
uavs.notes: Army ordered 33 systems (4 aircraft each) by March 2004 with plans for up to 88 systems. The RQ-7B introduced the Tactical Common Data Link (TCDL).

uavs.name: Fire Scout
uavs.role: Naval VTOL UAV — Reconnaissance, Targeting, Armed Scout

variants.description: Based on Schweizer 330SP; first LRIP May 2001
Based on Schweizer 333, 4-blade rotor; FCS UAS; only 30 bought; retired Oct 2022
Based on Bell 407; larger, 15h endurance, IOC June 2019; 38 built, retiring by 2026

uavs.description: Fire Scout is an autonomous shipboard VTOL UAV selected by the U.S. Navy in 2000 to replace the RQ-2 Pioneer. The RQ-8A was based on the Schweizer 330SP and first flew in January 2000. The MQ-8B was selected for the Army's FCS UAS and Navy's LCS but the Army cancelled in 2010. Only 30 MQ-8Bs were acquired by the Navy, retiring October 2022. The larger MQ-8C (Bell 407-based) achieved IOC June 2019; 38 were built but the Navy plans retirement by 2026.
uavs.notes: The MQ-8B demonstrated armed rocket operations from LCS. MQ-8C provides 3x the endurance of MQ-8B.

uavs.name: Reaper (Predator B)

images.caption: MQ-9A Reaper
images.credit: Senior Airman Larry E. Reid Jr., USAF

variants.description: Reaper — primary USAF variant; 366 ordered, 300+ delivered
Extended Range — external wing tanks, reinforced gear; 38 upgraded, up to 33h ISR endurance
Predator B ER — longer wing, civil airspace compliant; first user: RAF Protector RG.1
Maritime variant — sonobuoy, ASW, mine-laying kits; 2200 km (1200 nm) radius
Modified wing/tail kit for STOL from expeditionary bases / amphibious ships

armament: AIM-9X Sidewinder (Block 2, vs cruise-missile threats, confirmed Sept 2020)
uavs.description: The Reaper (Predator B) is a larger, more capable successor to the MQ-1 Predator, developed as a private venture by GA-ASI from 1998, initially funded by NASA. Significantly more powerful turboprop engine gives it 50,000 ft ceiling and 27h endurance. Designated MQ-9A and named Reaper in 2006. Combat debut 2007 over Afghanistan. Used extensively in Afghanistan, Iraq, Yemen, Syria. USAF lost 12+ Reapers over Yemen in 2023/24 to Houthi SAMs. Over 300 delivered to USAF; retirement planned 2035. MQ-9B SkyGuardian first ordered by RAF, first RAF deliveries late 2023.
uavs.notes: The MQ-9B STOL variant with foldable wings is designed for LHA-type carrier deck operations. The SeaGuardian can conduct ASW missions via sonobuoy dispensing.

uavs.name: SnowGoose
uavs.role: Autonomous Cargo Delivery / ISR Parafoil UAV
uavs.description: The SnowGoose is a powered parafoil UAV procured for U.S. Special Operations Command (SOCOM) for clandestine cargo delivery and ISR missions. It can be air-launched from an aircraft and autonomously delivers supplies or equipment to designated coordinates. The 'C' prefix in the designation indicates its cargo/transport mission.
uavs.notes: The parafoil design allows quiet, low-speed flight suitable for covert logistics support in denied areas.

uavs.name: Raven
uavs.role: Man-portable Small UAV — Local Reconnaissance & Force Protection

variants.description: Block II — official designation allocated late 2004; hand-launched
Raven B — lighter GCS, laser illuminator, improved sensors; won Army SUAV competition 2005

uavs.description: The Raven is a small hand-launched UAV developed from AeroVironment's FQM-151 Pointer under the Army's Pathfinder ACTD program. The proof-of-concept 'Flashlight' flew October 2001. Block I was delivered May 2003; Block II (RQ-11A) evaluated in Afghanistan and used in Operation Iraqi Freedom. Over 1,300 built by early 2005, 2,000+ more on contract. SOCOM purchased 179 systems (3 vehicles each). Over 19,000 have been produced for U.S. and allied militaries — one of the most widely used military UAVs in the world. A single soldier can operate it.
uavs.notes: Operates at 30–300 m altitude. Can autonomously return to launch point with a single button press. Used for MOUT reconnaissance.

uavs.name: Wasp AE

variants.description: Wasp AE (All Environment) — waterproof, can land on water

uavs.description: The Wasp AE (All Environment) is an ultra-small man-portable UAV for squad-level reconnaissance. Waterproofed for maritime environments, it can be hand-launched and can land on water for recovery — the first small UAS approved by the FAA for commercial over-water operations. It streams live EO/IR video.
uavs.notes: AE = All Environment, referring to its waterproof capability for maritime/littoral operations.

uavs.name: Unknown / Unallocated
uavs.description: The Q-13 designation slot appears to be reserved or classified. No public information is available about this designation.
uavs.notes: Listed in the official index as a designation that has been allocated but for which no public details exist.

uavs.name: Dragon Eye / Swift
uavs.role: Small Backpackable UAV — Marine Corps ISR

variants.description: Dragon Eye — twin-motor backpackable UAV for USMC

uavs.description: The Dragon Eye was a small backpackable UAV developed for the U.S. Marine Corps for squad-level reconnaissance. It can be hand-launched and is quiet enough for clandestine operations. It was developed under the USMC's Small Unit Remote Scouting System (SURSS) program.
uavs.notes: Dragon Eye paved the way for the larger RQ-20 Puma AE in Marine Corps service.

uavs.name: Neptune
uavs.description: Neptune is a small ship-launched UAV developed by DRS Technologies for maritime reconnaissance. It is electrically powered, quiet, and suitable for operation from surface combatants.
uavs.notes: Limited information available publicly.

uavs.name: T-Hawk (Tarantula Hawk)
uavs.role: Micro Air Vehicle — Urban ISR / Close Reconnaissance

variants.description: T-Hawk — single ducted fan VTOL MAV

uavs.description: T-Hawk (Tarantula Hawk) is a VTOL micro air vehicle with a ducted fan design — no exposed rotor blades, enabling safe urban/indoor use. Developed under the U.S. Army's MAV program. Notably used at Fukushima Daiichi in April 2011 to inspect damaged nuclear reactor buildings, providing video imagery of the disaster zone.
uavs.notes: T-Hawks were also used for IED detection and EOD in Iraq and Afghanistan. The ducted fan allows hover without blade exposure hazard.

uavs.name: SpyHawk
uavs.description: The SpyHawk was a long-endurance UAV developed by MTC Technologies. Limited public information is available. The program did not proceed to full production.
uavs.notes: MTC Technologies was acquired by BAE Systems in 2007.

uavs.name: Hummingbird (A160T)
uavs.description: The A160T Hummingbird is an unmanned helicopter featuring an Optimum-Speed Rotor (OSR) that varies rotor RPM for peak efficiency at different speeds and altitudes. It achieved world-record rotorcraft endurance. DARPA used it to test foliage-penetrating radar. Boeing acquired Frontier Systems in 2004.
uavs.notes: The OSR technology is the key innovation — conventional helicopters use constant rotor RPM, while the A160T varies it dynamically.

uavs.name: Aerosonde
uavs.role: Small Long-Endurance ISR / Maritime / Weather Reconnaissance

RQ-10 Mk4.8 HQ.description: FTUAS (Future Tactical UAS) Aerosonde Mk 4.8 HQ variant

variants.description: Aerosonde Mk 4.8 HQ — FTUAS (Future Tactical UAS) for USMC; out of sequence

uavs.description: The Aerosonde is a small long-endurance UAV originally designed for meteorological research. It made the first unmanned transatlantic flight in 1998. Textron's AAI division militarized it for ISR. The MQ-19 was selected for various U.S. services. The RQ-10 (out of sequence) is the Aerosonde Mk 4.8 HQ selected for the USMC Future Tactical UAS (FTUAS) to replace the RQ-7 Shadow.
uavs.notes: The Aerosonde has a track record in extreme weather reconnaissance, including hurricane penetration missions.

uavs.name: Puma AE
uavs.role: Small Tactical UAV — Maritime & Land ISR

variants.description: Puma AE (All Environment) — waterproof, water landing/recovery

uavs.description: Puma AE (All Environment) is a hand-launched UAV for maritime and land reconnaissance. Fully waterproof, it can be water-launched and water-recovered, making it ideal for naval operations from ships and boats. It became the first small UAS to receive FAA approval for commercial flight operations over water. Extensively used by SOCOM.
uavs.notes: The RQ-20B Puma LE adds significantly extended endurance. Widely exported to allied special operations forces.

uavs.name: Blackjack

variants.description: Blackjack — pneumatic rail launcher, skyhook recovery; 31 systems (5 UAVs each)

uavs.description: The Blackjack is a variant of Boeing/Insitu's Integrator UAV, winning the Navy STUAS competition in June 2010. First RQ-21A flight July 2012. Launched by pneumatic rail (sea or land) and recovered by the innovative 'skyhook' system — a vertical rope catches the wing, and the vehicle spins to the ground. Max payload 17.7 kg (39 lb), highly configurable. USMC declared IOC 2016; replaced RQ-7 Shadow in Marine service. By 2023, Navy/Marines had 31 systems (5 vehicles each).
uavs.notes: The skyhook recovery system eliminates the need for runways or arrest gear, allowing shipboard and austere-field operations.

uavs.name: Global Observer

RQ-22A.propulsion: Liquid hydrogen fuel cell + electric motors

uavs.description: The Global Observer is a massive hydrogen-powered UAV designed to fly at stratospheric altitude (65,000 ft) for up to one week without landing, acting as a 'pseudo-satellite.' It uses liquid hydrogen fuel cells for zero-emission propulsion. Lost during a flight test in April 2011. Program did not proceed to full production.
uavs.notes: The liquid hydrogen propulsion system gave it 7-day endurance potential, effectively a persistent satellite substitute for communications relay and ISR.

uavs.name: TigerShark
uavs.manufacturer: NASC (Naval Air Systems Command) / Northrop Grumman
uavs.description: TigerShark is a medium tactical UAV developed for the U.S. Navy. Limited public details are available.
uavs.notes: Competed with other platforms for Navy/Marine Corps TUAV requirements.

uavs.name: K-MAX
uavs.description: The K-MAX is a manned intermeshing-rotor helicopter converted to unmanned cargo operations by Kaman and Lockheed Martin. It was deployed to Afghanistan in 2011, conducting the first autonomous cargo resupply missions in combat, delivering over 1.5 million pounds of supplies. The 'C' prefix denotes its cargo mission.
uavs.notes: In Afghanistan, K-MAX reduced the need for dangerous supply convoy runs, saving lives. The intermeshing rotor design (no tail rotor) maximizes lift efficiency.

uavs.name: Stingray

variants.description: CBARS — Carrier-Based Aerial Refueling System redesign

sensors: Carrier equipment: folding wings, arrestor hook, sturdy gear
uavs.description: The Stingray is the U.S. Navy's first operational carrier-based UAV. It evolved from the troubled UCLASS (stealthy ISR/strike) program, redefined as CBARS (pure tanker) in 2016. Boeing won the contract in October 2018. First flight September 2019; first aerial refueling test June 2021; carrier deck trials December 2021. It can offload 7,250 kg of fuel at 930 km range, extending the carrier air wing's strike radius. Controlled by MD-5C (carrier), MD-5D (shore), MD-5E (embarkable) ground stations. 76 aircraft planned; IOC on CVN-71 Theodore Roosevelt planned 2026.
uavs.notes: By taking over the tanker role from F/A-18s, the MQ-25 frees up strike fighters for combat sorties, significantly extending carrier strike group reach.

uavs.name: Aerostar
uavs.description: Aerostar is an Israeli tactical UAV from Aeronautics Defense Systems, adopted by the U.S. Army under the RQ-26 designation for evaluation. It is a conventional twin-boom pusher design with good endurance characteristics.
uavs.notes: Widely exported; used operationally in multiple conflict zones including Ukraine.

uavs.name: ScanEagle

variants.description: ScanEagle — pneumatic catapult launch, skyhook or net recovery

uavs.description: ScanEagle is a long-endurance ISR UAV that can be launched from a pneumatic SuperWedge catapult and recovered using Insitu's SkyHook system. Originally developed for commercial fishery monitoring, militarized version adopted by US military. It operates from ships and land bases, providing 24h+ persistent surveillance. One of the most widely exported small UAVs globally.
uavs.notes: ScanEagle was used extensively in Iraq and Afghanistan for convoy protection and persistent area surveillance. Over 1,000 flight hours per month during peak operations.

uavs.name: X2D
uavs.role: Autonomous Quadcopter — Tactical ISR / Base Security

variants.description: X2D — advanced AI obstacle avoidance, man-portable

uavs.description: The Skydio X2D is a highly autonomous quadcopter UAV known for its AI-powered obstacle avoidance (6 cameras providing 360° coverage). Adopted by the U.S. Army for tactical reconnaissance and base security. Compact, man-portable, and can fly autonomously in complex environments including forests, buildings, and tunnels.
uavs.notes: Skydio's autonomous obstacle avoidance was its key selling point vs competitors. The drone can maintain target tracking even through complex terrain.

uavs.name: LEA
uavs.description: The LEA is a small UAV developed by Technology Service Corporation. Very limited public information is available.
uavs.notes: One of the less-documented designations in the Q-series.

uavs.name: Valiant
uavs.description: The Valiant is a long-endurance UAV developed by Griffon Aerospace. Limited public information is available.
uavs.notes: Griffon Aerospace also produces aerial targets and drone systems.

uavs.name: Avenger (Predator C)

MQ-20A.propulsion: Pratt & Whitney PW545B turbofan, 17.8 kN (4000 lbf)

variants.description: Jet-powered, internal bay, some stealth features
Extended range variant with conformal fuel tanks

uavs.description: The Avenger (Predator C) is GA-ASI's jet-powered UCAV, developed as a private venture. It uses the same GCS as earlier Predator drones. Key stealth features include an internal weapons bay (1,360 kg total payload, 6 optional hardpoints) and S-shaped exhaust duct. First flew April 4, 2009. USAF evaluated one aircraft as 'MQ-X' successor to MQ-9 but cancelled in 2012, deeming cost/benefit insufficient. GA-ASI reported 7 sold to an undisclosed US government customer in 2017. Used for AI-based autonomous operations tests and new payload integration.
uavs.notes: Designation note: MQ-20A is out of sequence (Q-20 is the RQ-20 Puma AE). Neither MQ-20A nor prior YQ-11 informal designation is officially allocated. The number was taken from the F-20 parallel but applied informally by USAF and GA-ASI.

uavs.name: Ghost Bat

variants.description: Ghost Bat — first article built in Australia to receive a US MDS designation

uavs.description: The Ghost Bat is an advanced teaming UAV developed under Australia's Loyal Wingman program in partnership with Boeing. It is designed to fly autonomously alongside manned fighters (F/A-18F, F-35A) as a force multiplier, providing additional sensor coverage, jamming, and weapons carriage. The MQ-28A is historically significant as the first defense article designed and manufactured in Australia to receive a U.S. military MDS designation. First flew February 27, 2021.
uavs.notes: Three development aircraft built. The program demonstrated AI-enabled autonomous teaming with manned aircraft. A key element of the RAAF's plans for Advanced Air Superiority.

uavs.name: V-BAT

variants.description: V-BAT — single fan VTOL, tail-sitter design

uavs.description: The V-BAT is a tail-sitter VTOL UAV using a single ducted fan for both vertical lift and forward flight (no separate lift rotors). Developed by Martin UAV, acquired by Shield AI. Compact enough for ship-deck operations. Selected for U.S. Navy/Marine evaluation for maritime ISR. The 'M' prefix implies multi-mission capability including potential armed variants.
uavs.notes: The single ducted fan tail-sitter design is exceptionally simple mechanically — no swiveling rotors or tilting mechanisms needed for VTOL-to-cruise transition.

uavs.name: Dark Merlin
uavs.description: Dark Merlin is an attritable (expendable or reusable) air-launched UAV developed by General Atomics under USAF programs. The 'F' prefix in the designation indicates it is intended to be optionally expendable. Very limited public information is available.
uavs.notes: The FQ- designation prefix is new for the Q-series — 'F' indicates expendable/attritable vs standard recoverable UAV.

uavs.name: Fury
uavs.description: The Fury is an attritable air-launched combat UAV developed by Anduril Industries under USAF programs including OBSS (Off-Board Sensing Station). Designed for swarming operations when launched from larger aircraft, it can provide ISR or strike capability at low per-unit cost.
uavs.notes: Anduril is a leading defense tech company founded by Palmer Luckey. Fury is part of their autonomous systems portfolio.

uavs.name: Talon Blue

variants.description: Talon Blue — Northrop Grumman attritable UAV

uavs.description: Talon Blue is Northrop Grumman's attritable combat UAV for USAF programs. Part of the new generation of optionally expendable drones designed to operate in large numbers, supplementing manned aircraft. Very limited public information available.
uavs.notes: Part of the broader USAF Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA) ecosystem that also includes the XQ-58A Valkyrie.

uavs.name: Valkyrie
uavs.role: Experimental UCAV / Loyal Wingman / Attritable Strike UAV

images.caption: XQ-58A Valkyrie first flight
images.credit: 88 Air Base Wing Public Affairs, USAF

images.caption: XQ-58A Block 2
images.credit: Master Sgt. Tristan McIntire, 96th TW, USAF

variants.description: Planned USMC production variant for SEAD role (MUX TACAIR program)

armament: Payload: 270 kg internal + 270 kg wing stations
uavs.description: The Valkyrie is a low-cost attritable UCAV developed under AFRL's LCASD (Low Cost Attritable Strike Demonstrator) program. Kratos developed the XQ-222 design, designated XQ-58A in 2017. It is rocket-booster launched from a trailer and parachute-recovered — no runway needed. Range of 5,500 km on a small turbojet. Demonstrated: full performance envelope, SDB release from internal bay, data relay between F-35 and F-22, acted as loyal wingman. Flown autonomously by USAF AI Skyborg system in August 2023. USMC and Navy each purchased 2 for evaluation; Marines tested Electronic Attack payloads with F-35s. USMC plans MQ-58B for SEAD missions.
uavs.notes: Designation note: The number 58 was derived from the X-series (between X-57A and X-59A); there is no X-58A, and it is unclear why XQ-58A was used instead.

uavs.name: OBSS
uavs.description: The XQ-67A OBSS (Off-Board Sensing Station) is General Atomics' contribution to the USAF's collaborative combat aircraft ecosystem. It is designed to act as a distributed sensor node and/or weapons carrier operating off larger manned or unmanned platforms. First flew in 2024.
uavs.notes: The OBSS concept extends the sensor reach of manned aircraft like F-35s and F-22s by deploying networked attritable UAVs as remote sensing nodes.

uavs.name: Great Horned Owl
uavs.description: The RQ-72 Great Horned Owl is a classified Northrop Grumman UAV program. Very limited public information is available. The name follows the tradition of naming HALE/stealth ISR UAVs after birds of prey (like the Global Hawk, Fire Scout, etc.).
uavs.notes: Out-of-sequence designation suggests a distinct program priority or classification level.

uavs.name: SHEPARD
uavs.description: The RQ-73 SHEPARD is a classified Northrop Grumman UAV. SHEPARD is likely an acronym. Very limited public information is available.
uavs.notes: The consecutive numbering with RQ-72 suggests related or parallel programs at Northrop Grumman.

uavs.name: Sentinel

RQ-170A.note: All specs are estimates — USAF has published no official data

variants.description: Sentinel (likely full designation, never officially confirmed)

uavs.description: The Sentinel is a flying-wing low-observable UAV built by Lockheed Skunk Works. Development began post-9/11 under USAF AFRCO requirement for a stealthy forward ISR platform. Based partly on the company-funded Manta design. Ground testing started early 2005; first flight December 2005. Deployed to Kandahar 2007 but not publicly acknowledged until December 2009. Key missions: gathered ISR data for Operation Neptune Spear (bin Laden raid), May 2011. Also flew reconnaissance over Iran. In December 2011, an RQ-170 came down in Iran (widely attributed to GPS spoofing by Iran, though this is disputed). 20-30 total built. Currently operated by 30th and 44th Recon Squadrons at Creech AFB.
uavs.notes: Designation note: The number 170 was derived from Lockheed's in-house P-170 project number. Unofficially nicknamed 'Wraith.' The 'Beast of Kandahar' was its first public name.
